text: What is the default speed limit in an urban area in New Zealand?
optionA: 50 km/h
optionB: 60 km/h
explanation: The default urban speed limit in NZ is 50 km/h unless otherwise signed.

text: What is the open road speed limit in New Zealand?
optionA: 90 km/h
optionB: 100 km/h
explanation: The default open road speed limit in NZ is 100 km/h.

text: What does a red traffic light mean?
optionA: Slow down
optionB: Stop if safe to do so
explanation: A red light means you must stop and wait until the light turns green.

text: What does a yellow traffic light mean?
optionA: Speed up to get through
optionB: Stop if it is safe to do so
optionC: Slow down and prepare to stop
explanation: A yellow light means stop if it is safe to do so. Do not speed up.

text: At an uncontrolled intersection, who must give way?
optionA: The vehicle on the left
optionB: The vehicle on the right
optionD: The vehicle turning right must give way to oncoming traffic
explanation: When turning right, you must give way to oncoming traffic and vehicles turning left.

text: What is the legal blood alcohol limit for drivers under 20 in NZ?
optionA: Zero
optionB: 50mg per 100ml
explanation: Drivers under 20 must have zero alcohol in their blood or breath.

text: How far from a fire hydrant must you park?
optionA: 1 metre
optionB: 3 metres
explanation: You must not park within 6 metres of a fire hydrant.

text: When driving at night, when should you use your headlights?
optionA: Only when it is completely dark
optionB: From 30 minutes after sunset to 30 minutes before sunrise
optionD: Only when other cars are present
explanation: You must use headlights from 30 minutes after sunset to 30 minutes before sunrise.

text: When should you use high beam headlights at night?
optionA: Always at night
optionB: Only in the city
optionC: When no oncoming vehicles are within 100 metres
explanation: Use high beam when no oncoming vehicle is within 100 metres to avoid blinding other drivers.

text: What should you do if you feel drowsy while driving at night?
optionA: Open the window and keep driving
optionB: Turn up the music
optionD: Drive faster to reach your destination sooner
explanation: If you feel drowsy, pull over safely and rest. Fatigue is a major cause of crashes.

text: What shape is a 'Give Way' sign in New Zealand?
optionA: Octagon
optionB: Downward-pointing (inverted) triangle
explanation: Give Way signs in NZ are an inverted (downward-pointing) triangle with a red border.

text: What shape and colour is a Stop sign?
optionA: A yellow diamond
optionB: A blue circle
explanation: Stop signs are red octagons. You must come to a complete stop, even if no other traffic is present.

text: What does a round sign with a red border and a red diagonal line through it generally indicate?
optionA: A warning of a hazard ahead
optionB: A prohibition, such as no entry
optionC: A mandatory direction you must follow
explanation: Red circular signs with a diagonal line indicate something is prohibited, such as no entry or no stopping.

text: What do round blue signs with white symbols generally mean?
optionA: They give general information only
optionB: They warn of a hazard ahead
optionC: They show a mandatory instruction you must follow
explanation: Round blue signs give mandatory instructions, such as a compulsory turn or a minimum speed, that you must obey.

text: What shape are most warning signs, such as those for bends or roadworks?
optionA: Yellow diamond
optionB: Red octagon
explanation: Warning signs alerting drivers to hazards ahead are typically yellow, diamond-shaped signs.

text: What does an 'LSZ' sign (Limited Speed Zone) mean?
optionA: The speed limit no longer applies
optionB: You may drive up to the posted limit but must slow down further if conditions require
optionC: You must always drive at exactly the posted limit
explanation: An LSZ sign means the speed limit shown is a maximum only — you must reduce your speed further if road, weather, or traffic conditions require it.

text: What does a sign with a yellow school-children symbol and a variable speed display usually indicate?
optionA: A permanent 100 km/h zone
optionB: A school speed zone, often 40 km/h during set times
optionD: A pedestrian crossing with no speed restriction
explanation: School zone signs indicate a reduced speed limit, commonly 40 km/h, that applies during the times shown or when the sign is flashing.

text: At a railway level crossing with a crossbuck sign but no lights or bells, what must you do?
optionA: Proceed without slowing if no train is visible
optionB: Sound your horn and continue
optionC: Slow down, look both ways, and give way to any train
optionD: Stop only if another vehicle is also crossing
explanation: At a railway crossing marked only with a crossbuck sign, you must slow down, check both directions, and give way to any approaching train.

text: What is the speed limit when passing a stopped school bus with its warning lights flashing (from either direction, without a median strip)?
optionA: 20 km/h
optionB: 30 km/h
explanation: You must slow to 20 km/h when passing a school bus that has stopped with its flashing lights on, from either direction, unless separated by a median strip.

text: What is the speed limit for a vehicle towing a trailer on the open road, unless a lower limit is signed?
optionA: 70 km/h
optionB: 80 km/h
explanation: A vehicle and trailer combination must not exceed 90 km/h, even where the open road limit of 100 km/h applies.

text: In a Limited Speed Zone (LSZ), what does the posted speed limit represent?
optionA: The speed you must always drive at
optionB: The minimum speed allowed
optionC: A maximum that you must reduce below if conditions require
optionD: A recommendation only, with no legal force
explanation: In an LSZ, the posted limit is a maximum — drivers must still slow down further if the road, weather, or traffic conditions make the posted speed unsafe.

text: If there is no speed limit sign displayed on a rural road, what speed limit applies by default?
optionA: 80 km/h
optionB: 90 km/h
explanation: Where no sign is displayed, the default open road speed limit of 100 km/h applies.

text: When driving through a temporary roadworks area with a lower speed limit sign posted, what must you do?
optionA: Ignore it if no workers are visible
optionB: Obey the temporary speed limit shown
optionC: Only slow down if other cars are slowing
optionD: Drive at the normal open road limit
explanation: Temporary speed limits at roadworks are legally enforceable and must be obeyed, whether or not workers are visible at the time.

text: What is the speed limit in a designated shared/pedestrian zone where a 10 km/h sign is posted?
optionA: 10 km/h
optionB: 20 km/h
explanation: Shared zones designed for pedestrians and vehicles together are often signed at 10 km/h to keep pedestrians safe.

text: Is it an offence to drive unreasonably slowly and hold up other traffic without good reason?
optionA: No, you can drive as slowly as you like
optionB: Yes, driving unreasonably slowly and obstructing traffic is an offence
optionD: Only if you are towing a trailer
explanation: Drivers must not unreasonably obstruct the movement of other traffic by driving unnecessarily slowly.

text: What is a permanent variable school speed zone speed limit typically set to during school pick-up and drop-off times?
optionA: 30 km/h
optionB: 40 km/h
explanation: Variable school speed zones commonly reduce the limit to 40 km/h during the signed times to protect children near schools.

text: At an uncontrolled T-intersection, who must give way?
optionA: The vehicle on the continuing (through) road
optionB: The vehicle on the terminating road (the top of the T)
explanation: At a T-intersection, traffic on the terminating road must give way to traffic on the continuing through road.

text: When entering a roundabout, who must you give way to?
optionA: Vehicles waiting to enter from other approaches
optionB: Vehicles already on the roundabout, coming from your right
optionD: No one, roundabouts have no give way rules
explanation: You must give way to any vehicle already circulating on the roundabout, which will be approaching from your right.

text: At a marked pedestrian (zebra) crossing, what must a driver do if a pedestrian is on the crossing?
optionA: Sound the horn to warn them
optionB: Give way and let the pedestrian finish crossing
optionD: Only stop if they are running
explanation: Drivers must give way to pedestrians who are on or entering a marked pedestrian crossing.

text: If a bus is indicating to pull out from a bus stop and it is safe to do so, what should you do?
optionA: Speed up to pass before it pulls out
optionB: Give way and let the bus merge back into traffic
optionC: Ignore it, buses have no priority
explanation: Drivers must give way to a bus indicating to pull out from a bus stop, provided it is safe for the bus to do so.

text: When an emergency vehicle is approaching with its siren or flashing lights on, what should you do?
optionA: Speed up to clear the intersection first
optionB: Move as far left as safely possible, and slow down or stop to let it pass
optionD: Ignore it unless it is behind you
explanation: You must give way to emergency vehicles using a siren or flashing lights by moving left and slowing or stopping to let them pass safely.

text: When your lane comes to an end and merges with another lane, who should give way?
optionA: The vehicle in the continuing lane
optionB: The vehicle in the ending lane
optionD: Neither, both proceed at the same time
explanation: A driver in a lane that is ending must give way to traffic already in the lane they are merging into.

text: What must a driver do when facing a Give Way sign at an intersection?
optionA: Come to a complete stop regardless of traffic
optionB: Slow down and give way to traffic on the road being entered or crossed
optionC: Proceed if no vehicles are visible from the left
explanation: A Give Way sign means you must slow down, and if necessary stop, to give way to traffic on the road you are entering or crossing.

text: A driver turning left across a marked cycle lane must give way to:
optionA: Nothing, turning traffic always has priority
optionB: A cyclist travelling straight ahead in the cycle lane
optionD: Only cyclists travelling faster than 20 km/h
explanation: When turning across a cycle lane, drivers must give way to cyclists continuing straight through in that lane.

text: Two vehicles arrive from opposite directions: one turning right, one turning left, both into the same street. Who gives way?
optionA: The vehicle turning left gives way to the vehicle turning right
optionB: The vehicle turning right gives way to the vehicle turning left
explanation: When a vehicle turning right and a vehicle turning left from the opposite direction are both turning into the same street, the vehicle turning right must give way to the vehicle turning left.

text: How close to an intersection without traffic signals are you allowed to park, unless a parking space is marked?
optionA: Not within 1 metre
optionB: Not within 3 metres
explanation: Unless a parking space is marked, you must not stop within 6 metres of an intersection without traffic signals.

text: How close to a pedestrian crossing (on the approach side) must you avoid stopping?
optionA: Within 1 metre
optionB: Within 3 metres
explanation: You must not stop within 6 metres of a pedestrian crossing on the approach side, to keep sightlines clear for pedestrians and drivers.

text: Is it legal to park alongside another vehicle that is already parked at the kerb (double parking)?
optionA: Yes, as long as your hazard lights are on
optionB: No, double parking is not allowed
optionD: Yes, if it is outside business hours
explanation: Double parking — stopping alongside a vehicle already parked at the kerb — is illegal because it obstructs traffic.

text: Can you legally park on a footpath or grass berm?
optionA: Yes, anywhere
optionB: No, unless specifically permitted by signage or markings
optionC: Yes, if only two wheels are on it
explanation: Parking on a footpath or berm is not allowed unless signs or markings specifically permit it.

text: Who is allowed to park in a mobility parking space?
optionA: Anyone, if the space is empty
optionB: Only vehicles displaying a valid mobility parking permit
optionC: Any vehicle for up to 15 minutes
explanation: Mobility parks are reserved for vehicles displaying a valid mobility parking permit.

text: What are loading zones reserved for?
optionA: Any vehicle parking overnight
optionB: Vehicles actively loading or unloading goods
explanation: Loading zones are reserved for vehicles that are actively loading or unloading goods, usually within a set time limit.

text: What does a clearway sign mean?
optionA: You may stop briefly to drop off passengers
optionB: No stopping is allowed at any time the clearway is in effect, even briefly
optionC: Parking is free during the signed hours
optionD: Only buses may use the lane
explanation: On a clearway, stopping is prohibited during the signed hours, even to briefly drop off or pick up passengers.

text: Can you stop your vehicle on a bridge or in a tunnel?
optionA: Yes, if there is no other traffic
optionB: No, unless a parking space is specifically marked there
optionD: Yes, if hazard lights are used
explanation: You must not stop on a bridge, in a tunnel, or in an underpass unless a parking space is specifically marked.

text: Before opening a car door after parking, what must a driver do?
optionA: Open it quickly to avoid delaying other traffic
optionB: Check it is safe and will not endanger cyclists or other road users
explanation: Drivers and passengers must check it is safe before opening a door, so as not to endanger cyclists or other road users passing by.

text: What is the legal breath alcohol limit for drivers aged 20 and over in New Zealand?
optionA: 150 micrograms per litre of breath
optionB: 250 micrograms per litre of breath
optionD: There is no legal breath limit
explanation: For drivers 20 and over, the legal breath alcohol limit is 250 micrograms of alcohol per litre of breath.

text: What is the legal blood alcohol limit for drivers aged 20 and over in New Zealand?
optionA: 20mg per 100ml
optionB: 50mg per 100ml
explanation: For drivers 20 and over, the legal blood alcohol limit is 50mg of alcohol per 100ml of blood.

text: What happens if a driver refuses to undergo a breath or blood test when required by Police?
optionA: Nothing, refusal has no consequence
optionB: It is treated as an offence, similar to failing the test
optionD: They can request a different officer instead
explanation: Refusing to undergo a required breath or blood test is itself an offence and is treated as seriously as failing the test.

text: Is it legal to drive if your ability is impaired by drugs, including prescription medication?
optionA: Yes, if the drug was legally prescribed
optionB: No, driving while impaired by any drug is illegal
optionC: Yes, as long as you drive under 50 km/h
optionD: Only illegal for recreational drugs, not prescription ones
explanation: It is illegal to drive while impaired by any drug, whether illegal or legally prescribed, if it affects your ability to drive safely.

text: What is the purpose of an alcohol interlock device?
optionA: It records your speed for insurance purposes
optionB: It prevents the vehicle from starting if alcohol is detected on the driver's breath
optionC: It automatically calls Police if you speed
optionD: It is a device fitted to breathalyse passengers
explanation: An alcohol interlock device requires a breath sample before the vehicle will start, preventing it from starting if alcohol is detected. It can be required after certain drink-driving convictions.

text: Which drivers must have a zero alcohol limit while driving in New Zealand?
optionA: Only drivers over 65
optionB: Drivers under 20, and drivers subject to an alcohol interlock licence condition
optionD: No one, the limit is the same for everyone
explanation: Drivers under 20 and drivers on an alcohol interlock licence condition must have zero alcohol in their breath or blood while driving.

text: If you are supervising a learner driver, what alcohol limit applies to you?
optionA: You may drink as normal, only the learner is restricted
optionB: You must not exceed the legal alcohol limit that applies to a supervising driver
optionC: There is no limit for supervisors
optionD: Only illegal drugs are restricted for supervisors
explanation: A person supervising a learner driver must also comply with the legal alcohol limit, as they are responsible for the vehicle's safe operation.

text: How does alcohol generally affect a driver's ability?
optionA: It has no effect at low amounts
optionB: It slows reaction time and impairs judgement
optionC: It only affects vision, not reaction time
optionD: It improves concentration in small amounts
explanation: Alcohol slows reaction time, impairs judgement and coordination, all of which increase the risk of a crash.

text: How does combining alcohol with fatigue affect driving risk?
optionA: It has no additional effect
optionB: It significantly increases the risk, more than either factor alone
optionC: Fatigue cancels out the effects of alcohol
explanation: Fatigue and alcohol both impair driving ability, and combining the two significantly increases the risk of a crash compared with either alone.

text: When following another vehicle at night, when should you dip your headlights?
optionA: Only if the vehicle ahead flashes its lights
optionB: Within 100 metres, to avoid dazzling the driver ahead via their mirrors
optionC: You never need to dip your lights when following
optionD: Only if driving faster than 80 km/h
explanation: You should dip your headlights within 100 metres of a vehicle you are following, to avoid dazzling the driver through their mirrors.

text: Should you wear sunglasses or heavily tinted glasses while driving at night?
optionA: Yes, they reduce glare from other headlights
optionB: No, they further reduce your already limited visibility at night
explanation: Sunglasses or heavily tinted glasses reduce the small amount of light available at night and should not be worn while night driving.

text: When should you use fog lights?
optionA: Every time you drive at night
optionB: Only when visibility is seriously reduced by fog, heavy rain, or dust
explanation: Fog lights should only be used when visibility is seriously reduced by conditions such as fog, heavy rain, or dust, not for ordinary night driving.

text: What lighting must a cyclist have when riding at night?
optionA: A red front light and white rear light only
optionB: A white front light, a red rear light, and a rear reflector, visible from a distance
optionC: No lighting is required if riding on footpaths
optionD: Only a rear reflector is required
explanation: At night, a cyclist must display a white light at the front and a red light at the rear, visible from a reasonable distance, along with a red rear reflector.

text: When must a motorcycle have its headlight switched on?
optionA: Only at night
optionB: At all times, day and night
explanation: Motorcycles must have their headlights switched on at all times, both during the day and at night, to improve their visibility to other road users.

text: Why should drivers be especially alert for animals on rural roads at night?
optionA: Animals are more active and harder to see in low light, especially around dawn and dusk
optionB: Animals cannot be legally on rural roads
optionC: It is only a concern during the day
optionD: Animals are attracted to headlights and will avoid the road
explanation: Many animals are most active around dawn, dusk, and at night, and are harder to see in low light, so extra caution is needed on rural roads.

text: At night, how should you judge a safe driving speed?
optionA: Always drive at the posted daytime speed limit
optionB: Drive at a speed that lets you stop within the distance you can see to be clear, which may be less than the posted limit
optionC: Speed does not need to change at night
optionD: Only reduce speed if it is raining
explanation: At night your visibility is reduced, so you should drive at a speed that allows you to stop within the distance illuminated by your headlights, even if that is below the posted limit.
